We are asked to generate the complete Ascend C artifacts for a kernel named `relu_custom` (PascalCase: `ReluCustom`). The original model is a simple ReLU activation. We need to produce six strings: project_json_src, host_tiling_src, host_operator_src, kernel_src, python_bind_src, model_src. The kernel function name in kernel_src must be `relu_custom`. The operator definition in project_json_src and host_operator_src should correspond to `ReluCustom`.

We will adapt the leaky_relu example to ReLU. ReLU is simpler: no negative slope parameter. So the operator has one input tensor x, one output tensor y, no attributes. The computation: y = max(x, 0). In the kernel, we can use the Ascend C max function. We'll keep similar tiling and buffer management, but remove the negative slope. Also adjust the host_operator_src to have no attribute. The python_bind_src should call aclnnReluCustom. The model_src will import custom_ops_lib and call the function `relu_custom`.

Note: The original leaky_relu example used a custom tiling with UB budget, block dim, etc. We'll replicate that structure for ReLU, but simplified: no negative slope attribute. We'll set tile length similarly. Also, the kernel function signature: extern "C" __global__ __aicore__ void relu_custom(GM_ADDR x, GM_ADDR y, GM_ADDR workspace, GM_ADDR tiling). The TILING_DATA will have totalLength, tileLength, blockDim (no negativeSlope). The Init will take x, y, totalLength, tileLength. The Compute will do: AscendC::Max(yLocal, xLocal, 0.0f, len); or we can use Mins if we want to compute negative part but for ReLU it's just max(0). Actually ReLU = max(x, 0). So we can use AscendC::Max. Or we can use formula: y = x * (x > 0) but Max is fine.

We must ensure all six strings are properly generated. Also the python_bind_src uses TORCH_LIBRARY_IMPL(myops, PrivateUse1, m) and m.impl("relu_custom", ...). PYBIND11_MODULE. The model_src: import custom_ops_lib; _FN = 'relu_custom'; class ModelNew: forward calls getattr(custom_ops_lib, _FN)(*inputs). Note that the original forward takes x only; negative_slope is removed.

We'll produce clean code. No extra text.
